html:
Brain Development and Malformations

Neuroembryology and Developmental Timing

Understanding when in development a process occurs is the conceptual foundation for why malformations look the way they do. The brain builds itself in a strictly ordered sequence — each stage depends on the fidelity of the stage before it. Disruption at a given developmental window produces a characteristic pattern of malformation that is largely predictable from timing alone.

Two principles follow from this: (1) earlier disruption produces more severe and widespread malformation; (2) the imaging pattern of a malformation reflects its developmental stage of origin — recognizing where in the sequence things went wrong is both a diagnostic and a mechanistic exercise.

Table 1. Neuroembryological Developmental Timing Framework

GESTATIONAL AGEDOMINANT PROCESSKEY CNS EVENTSEXAMPLE DISORDERS (selected)
3–4 weeksPrimary neurulationNeural tube closure (rostral neuropore d24, caudal neuropore d26–28); neural crest migration; early spinal cord formationAnencephaly (failure rostral closure); myelomeningocele / spina bifida (failure caudal closure); encephalocele; Chiari II (deformation sequence secondary to MMC)
4–6 weeksTelencephalization / early forebrain inductionForebrain cleavage into paired hemispheres; optic and olfactory placode formation; early posterior fossa formation; facial midline development (SHH-driven)Holoprosencephaly (failure of forebrain cleavage — alobar→lobar); Dandy-Walker spectrum; craniosynostosis (cranial suture dysregulation); cyclopia/midface anomalies in severe HPE
6–16 weeksNeuronal and glial proliferationSymmetric divisions in ventricular zone → neurons and glia; asymmetric divisions → postmitotic neuroblasts; programmed apoptosis; early brain volume expansionPrimary microcephaly (MCPH genes: ASPM, CDK5RAP2 — reduced proliferation); megalencephaly (PIK3CA, PTEN, AKT3 mTOR pathway — excessive proliferation); hemimegalencephaly
12–24 weeksNeuronal migrationRadial migration along Bergmann glia (inside-out lamination); tangential migration of interneurons; corpus callosum formation (genu first, then body, splenium, then rostrum last); gyral pattern formationLissencephaly/pachygyria (LIS1, DCX, TUBA1A); band heterotopia (DCX females); periventricular nodular heterotopia (FLNA); polymicrogyria (many causes); ACC/dysgenesis; schizencephaly
24 weeks – postnatalCortical organization and synaptogenesisLate neuronal migration; dendritic arborization; synaptogenesis; axonal pruning; programmed cell death; establishment of columnar organizationFocal cortical dysplasia types I–III (subtle organization failure; MTOR somatic); mild cortical dysplasia; autism-associated cortical dysregulation
24 weeks – 2 yearsMyelinationOligodendrocyte maturation; myelin deposition (peripheral → central; posterior → anterior; caudal → rostral); vulnerability to destructive processes peaks in preterm WMHypomyelination disorders (PMD/PLP1, HABC); leukodystrophies; periventricular leukomalacia (preterm WM injury); post-injury WM loss
Corpus Callosum Development — A Brief Note on Sequence
The CC forms in a specific order: genu → body → splenium → rostrum. The rostrum is the LAST part to form despite being the most anterior structure.
This matters clinically: the part of the CC that is absent predicts the timing (and often severity) of the insult.
If only the genu is present: severe early disruption (≤12–14 weeks). If genu and body are present but splenium and rostrum absent: mid-gestational disruption. Isolated rostrum absence: late, mild disruption.
A thin but complete CC (all segments present) is different from partial agenesis and is more often seen with prematurity, metabolic disorders, or diffuse injury than with a specific developmental malformation.

Corpus Callosum Malformations

ACC and related CC malformations are among the most common brain malformations detected on prenatal and postnatal imaging. Their clinical significance is highly variable and poorly predicted by imaging alone — genetic testing is fairly low-yield in isolated ACC but when combined with other developmental brain malformations, congenital anomalies, or neurodevelopmental abnormalities, genetic testing should be strongly considered.

Table 2. Corpus Callosum Malformations

MALFORMATIONIMAGING FEATURESCLINICAL / GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS
Complete ACCAbsent CC on all sequences; parallel ('racing car') ventricles; colpocephaly (posterior > anterior horn dilation); interhemispheric cysts common; absent cingulate gyrus → gyri radiate from roof of 3rd ventricle ('sunburst' or 'spokes of wheel' pattern on coronal)Highly heterogeneous outcome: ~20% of isolated ACC detected prenatally has normal neurodevelopment; ~50% have intellectual disability or ASD features when detected postnatally due to ascertainment bias. Genetic workup essential: chromosomal microarray + WES. Associated: Aicardi syndrome (girls: ACC + chorioretinal lacunae + infantile spasms), Andermann syndrome (ACC + peripheral neuropathy, SLC12A6), L1CAM (X-linked, males).
Partial / hypogenetic ACCPartial absence — identify which segments are present. Developmental order: genu → body → splenium → rostrum (rostrum is LAST despite being anterior). Therefore: if only genu present → severe, early disruption. If genu + body present but no splenium/rostrum → mid-gestational. Rostrum absence alone = mild/late.The 'which part is missing predicts timing of insult' rule applies. Partial ACC carries similar genetic heterogeneity to complete ACC. Isolated thin/hypoplastic CC (all segments present but thin) may be seen in prematurity, metabolic disorders, or as an isolated finding — clinical significance context-dependent.
Callosal dysgenesis with interhemispheric cystACC or partial ACC + midline cyst that may or may not communicate with ventricles. Cyst can cause hydrocephalus or mass effect.Associated with chromosomal anomalies (especially 8p inverted duplication). Neurosurgical referral if cyst causes hydrocephalus or progressive mass effect.
ACC — Practical Points for Residents
Isolated ACC on prenatal MRI: counsel families that ~20% have normal neurodevelopment — but postnatal surveillance is essential as cognitive and social difficulties often emerge in school age.
The 'sunburst' or 'radial' gyral pattern on coronal MRI (gyri radiating from 3rd ventricle roof) is a reliable sign of ACC — the cingulate sulcus fails to form, so gyri run perpendicular to the midline rather than parallel.
Probst bundles: longitudinally oriented WM bundles that run parallel to the interhemispheric fissure — the axons that 'should have crossed' but didn't. They indent the medial walls of the lateral ventricles and are well seen on axial MRI.
Always send chromosomal microarray and consider WES for ACC — genetic yield is ~30–40% with comprehensive workup.
Aicardi syndrome: girls only (X-linked dominant, lethal in males); ACC + chorioretinal lacunae (pathognomonic) + infantile spasms. Always examine the retina in a girl with ACC.

Forebrain Malformations: HPE and SOD

Holoprosencephaly (HPE) and septo-optic dysplasia (SOD) represent a clinical and genetic spectrum of forebrain induction failure. SOD is on the extreme end of the optic nerve hypoplasia spectrum. SHH signaling is the central developmental pathway — mutations in SHH and its downstream effectors (ZIC2, SIX3, PTCH1, CDON) account for a significant proportion of familial HPE, though most cases remain without a molecular diagnosis. Trisomy 13 is the most common chromosomal cause of alobar HPE.

Table 3. Holoprosencephaly Spectrum and Septo-Optic Dysplasia

MALFORMATIONKEY IMAGINGGENETICS / CAUSECLINICAL FEATURES / PEARLS
Alobar HPESingle monoventricle; fused thalami; absent falx, CC, olfactory bulbs, interhemispheric fissure; fused basal ganglia; holosphereSHH (most common), ZIC2, SIX3, PTCH1, CDON; Trisomy 13 (most common chromosomal cause); Trisomy 18Severe: profound intellectual disability, refractory epilepsy, endocrine dysfunction (DI, GH deficiency), temperature dysregulation. Associated with cyclopia/proboscis/midface anomalies. Prognosis very poor.
Semilobar HPEPartial interhemispheric fissure (posterior present, anterior absent); partial temporal/occipital lobe formation; rudimentary posterior CC may be present; fused frontal lobes and thalamiSame gene spectrum as alobar; chromosomal anomalies less common than alobarSevere-moderate: significant DD, epilepsy, endocrine dysfunction. Spasticity. Better survival than alobar.
Lobar HPENear-complete hemisphere separation; absent or hypoplastic genu/rostrum of CC; fused cingulate gyrus; absent olfactory bulbs/sulci common; mild frontal lobe fusionOften familial/genetic (SHH most common single-gene); may be isolated findingVariable: may have near-normal cognition or mild-moderate DD. Epilepsy, behavioral issues, hypothalamic dysfunction possible. Diagnosis sometimes not made until school age.
Middle interhemispheric (MIH) variantFailure of posterior frontal/parietal interhemispheric separation; anterior and posterior brain separated normally; absent body of CC (genu and splenium present)Genetic overlap with lobar HPE; some isolated casesMilder phenotype: hypotonia, mild motor delay, mild cognitive issues. Often underdiagnosed — requires careful review of CC and posterior frontal region.
Septo-optic dysplasia (SOD)
Extreme end of the optic nerve hypoplasia spectrum		Absent septum pellucidum (not universal) + small optic nerves/chiasm ± hypothalamic-pituitary axis anomalies ± cortical malformations (especially schizencephaly)SOD is a clinical-radiological diagnosis, not a single genetic entity. HESX1, SOX2, OTX2 account for minority of cases; most are sporadic. Young maternal age association.Triad: visual impairment (nystagmus, poor acuity) + pituitary hormone deficiencies (GH most common, then hypothyroidism, then panhypopituitarism, DI) + absent SP. Endocrine evaluation essential — pituitary dysfunction can be life-threatening and may not be apparent at birth. Absent SP alone does not diagnose SOD.
HPE — Clinical Pearls
HPE and facial anomalies: 'the face predicts the brain' — midline facial defects (cyclopia, proboscis, hypotelorism, median cleft palate, single central incisor) scale with HPE severity. A single central maxillary incisor in an otherwise normal-appearing child should prompt brain MRI.
SOD endocrine urgency: GH deficiency in infancy causes hypoglycemia, which can be life-threatening. Any child with SOD requires urgent endocrine evaluation, even if asymptomatic at diagnosis. The triple triad of SOD is often incomplete — absent SP alone does not make the diagnosis.
HPE genetics: SHH mutations in familial HPE show markedly variable expressivity — a parent may carry an HPE-causing SHH mutation with only a single central incisor and normal brain imaging, while their child has alobar HPE. Genetic counseling is essential.
ZIC2 HPE: thalami often more severely fused than expected for the degree of cortical non-separation (semi-lobar HPE with near-complete thalamic fusion). ZIC2 mutations are rarely associated with facial anomalies — important for prenatal counseling.

Neuronal Migration Disorders and Cortical Malformations

The dominant pathways disrupted in migration disorders are microtubule dynamics (LIS1, DCX, TUBA1A), dystroglycan glycosylation (cobblestone disorders), cytoskeletal actin regulation (FLNA → PNH), and mTOR pathway (PMG, HME, FCD). Recognizing which pathway is likely disrupted guides targeted gene panel selection.

Table 4. Neuronal Migration Disorders — Genetics, Mechanism, and Clinical Features

MALFORMATIONGENE(S)MECHANISMIMAGING GRADIENT / PATTERNCLINICAL / PEARLS
Classic lissencephaly (Type 1 / LCH)LIS1 (PAFAH1B1) DCX TUBA1A ARX (X-linked, males)Dynein-mediated radial migration failure; microtubule dysfunctionLIS1: posterior > anterior (P-A gradient). DCX: anterior > posterior (A-P gradient) in males. TUBA1A: variable, often with CC dysgenesis. ARX: lissencephaly + ambiguous genitalia (males).Severe: hypotonia at birth → spasticity; refractory epilepsy (infantile spasms, Lennox-Gastaut); severe-profound intellectual disability. DCX females: subcortical band heterotopia (milder phenotype). TUBA1A: often with brainstem and cerebellar hypoplasia.
Subcortical band heterotopia (SBH)DCX (X-linked, females) LIS1 (rare)Partial neuronal migration arrest — neurons stop mid-journeySmooth band of gray matter signal between ventricle and cortex; separated by thin WM layers; overlying cortex relatively normal or mildly simplifiedClassic DCX females: band heterotopia + mild-moderate epilepsy + variable cognitive outcomes (milder than lissencephaly males with same DCX mutation). Males with DCX: lissencephaly. Classic X-linked inheritance in family history is clue.
Periventricular nodular heterotopia (PNH)FLNA (X-linked) ARFGEF2 Many others (heterogeneous)Failure of neuronal departure from germinal matrix — neurons never begin migrationNodules isointense to gray matter on ALL sequences lining lateral ventricle walls; no signal change; bilateral or unilateralFLNA: X-linked, females (males often lethal prenatally); cardiovascular anomalies (patent ductus, valvular), connective tissue features. Epilepsy often in 2nd decade. IQ variable. Distinguish from TSC subependymal nodules (which are NOT isointense to cortex — T1-bright/FLAIR-bright).
Cobblestone lissencephaly (Type 2)POMT1, POMT2 POMGNT1 FKTN (Fukuyama) FKRP LARGE1Dystroglycanopathy — failure of α-dystroglycan O-mannosylation; disruption of glia limitans → overmigrationCobblestone cortex (irregular bumpy surface, cortex appears too thick with irregular outer margin); lissencephalic-appearing but mechanistically distinct. Often with brainstem/cerebellar hypoplasia and eye anomalies.Walker-Warburg (most severe): agyria + eye anomalies (microphthalmia, cataract, retinal dysplasia) + CMD; neonatal presentation; very poor prognosis. Fukuyama CMD (FKTN): common in Japan; CMD + cortical dysplasia; cardiomyopathy. Muscle-eye-brain (POMGNT1): intermediate. Serum CK often elevated. Send dystroglycanopathy panel.
Polymicrogyria (PMG)PIK3R2, TUBB2B, GPR56, COL18A1, ADGRV1 Also: TORCH, ischemia, metabolicPost-migrational cortical organization failure — multiple small fused gyri; unlayered cortexIrregular cortical surface; apparent cortical thickening on low-resolution; perisylvian most common distribution (bilateral perisylvian PMG syndrome). Mimics pachygyria on low-res MRI.Bilateral perisylvian PMG: pseudobulbar palsy, drooling, dysarthria + epilepsy ± cognitive impairment. PIK3R2: associated with megalencephaly-PMG-polydactyly-hydrocephalus (MPPH). TUBB2B: often asymmetric. Genetic testing yield ~30% — chromosomal microarray + targeted gene panel or WES.
Hemimegalencephaly (HME)Somatic mosaic: PIK3CA, AKT3, MTOR (germline rare)Somatic mTOR pathway activation in progenitor cells → excessive, dysplastic proliferation of one hemisphereUnilateral hemisphere overgrowth with abnormal signal; ipsilateral ventricle often enlarged and dysmorphic; cortical dysplasia throughout affected hemisphere; WM signal abnormal; midline shift variableMacrocephaly (asymmetric) or normal HC; contralateral hemiplegia; refractory focal epilepsy beginning in infancy — often epileptic encephalopathy. Hemispherotomy is the primary epilepsy treatment for medically refractory cases. FISH or SNP array on resected tissue for somatic mosaicism. Associated with epidermal nevus syndrome and other segmental overgrowth disorders.
mTOR Pathway — A Unifying Framework
The mTOR (mechanistic target of rapamycin) pathway regulates cell growth, proliferation, and differentiation. Germline gain-of-function mutations → megalencephaly syndromes (MCAP, MPPH). Somatic mosaic mutations → focal cortical dysplasia, hemimegalencephaly, focal overgrowth.
The somatic vs. germline distinction explains why HME and FCD can occur without a family history — the mutation is present only in a subset of cells.
mTOR inhibitors (rapamycin/everolimus) have demonstrated efficacy in TSC — rationale for clinical trials in other mTOR-related epilepsies.
When sending gene testing for FCD or HME, standard blood DNA may miss somatic variants with low allele fraction — brain tissue (surgical specimen) or saliva/urinary sediment for alternative cell sources improves yield.

Posterior Fossa Malformations and Joubert Syndrome

Posterior fossa malformations range from incidental variants (mega cisterna magna — see Neuroradiology chapter) to severe disorders with major neurodevelopmental and multiorgan implications. Joubert syndrome is the paradigmatic ciliopathy — understanding its pathogenesis contextualizes why a cerebellar malformation produces retinal dystrophy, renal disease, and hepatic fibrosis.

Table 5. Posterior Fossa Malformations

MALFORMATIONMRI HALLMARKGENETICSCLINICAL FEATURES / PEARLS
Joubert syndrome (JBTS) and related disorders (JSRD)Molar tooth sign (MTS): axial MRI shows elongated, thickened superior cerebellar peduncles + deepened interpeduncular fossa + vermian hypoplasia → resembles a molar tooth. Vermian hypoplasia on sagittal.Highly genetically heterogeneous: >40 genes (CC2D2A, CEP290, RPGRIP1L, AHI1, NPHP1, TMEM67, others). All encode proteins of the primary cilium or basal body. Autosomal recessive (most); X-linked rare (OFD1).Core features: hypotonia, oculomotor apraxia, neonatal breathing dysregulation (episodic hyperpnea/apnea — pathognomonic when present), developmental delay, ataxia.
The MTS is required for diagnosis — it is not seen in Dandy-Walker or simple vermian hypoplasia.
Ciliopathy framework: primary cilia coordinate developmental signaling (Hedgehog, Wnt, PDGF). Dysfunction → multiorgan disease. Evaluate ALL Joubert patients for: retinal dystrophy (Leber congenital amaurosis / NPHP-related — ophthalmology); nephronophthisis (renal ultrasound + creatinine annually); hepatic fibrosis (LFTs + ultrasound); polydactyly (Meckel-Gruber overlap in severe forms).
Genotype-phenotype correlations exist: CEP290 → higher risk of Leber CGA; TMEM67 → hepatic fibrosis (COACH syndrome); AHI1 → retinal involvement; NPHP1 → renal disease.
Send JSRD/Joubert gene panel or WES — single-gene testing is low yield given heterogeneity.
Dandy-Walker malformation (DWM)Vermian hypoplasia + rotation; cystic dilation of 4th ventricle; enlarged posterior fossa; elevated tentorium/torcula (torcular-lambdoid inversion). Hydrocephalus common.Chromosomal anomalies in ~30% (monosomy X, Trisomy 18, 13, 21). Single gene causes: ZIC1/ZIC4 deletions (3q); FOXC1; many others. Often no genetic cause found.Prognosis determined primarily by associated anomalies (ACC, cortical malformations, chromosomal) — not by DWM itself. Isolated DWM with normal remaining brain: variable outcome, often reasonable. Hydrocephalus requires monitoring; CSF diversion may be needed. Do not conflate with mega cisterna magna or Blake's pouch cyst (see neuroradiology chapter).
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia (PCH)Small pons and cerebellum — variable pattern. Type 2 (TSEN genes): 'dragonfly' cerebellar pattern (hypoplastic hemispheres with relatively preserved vermis). Type 1 (VRK1, exosome complex): cerebellar hypoplasia + anterior horn disease (SMA-like).Heterogeneous: TSEN54 (most common, PCH2/4); RARS2 (PCH6, mitochondrial aminoacyl-tRNA); VRK1; CASK (X-linked, females, microcephaly + PCH). Autosomal recessive predominates.Severe neurodevelopmental impairment, microcephaly (often progressive), early-onset epilepsy, feeding difficulty, spasticity. PCH1 has additional anterior horn cell degeneration. Poor prognosis in most types. Brief coverage here — full PCH classification is complex; refer to Namavar et al. for detailed subtyping.
Joubert Syndrome — Multiorgan Surveillance Protocol
Ophthalmology: ERG and dilated fundus exam at diagnosis and annually. CEP290 mutations → high risk for Leber congenital amaurosis (may be treatable with gene therapy).
Renal: Renal ultrasound + creatinine/BUN at diagnosis and annually. Nephronophthisis (NPHP1 deletion most common) typically presents in adolescence with polyuria/polydipsia and progressive CKD.
Liver: LFTs + abdominal ultrasound at diagnosis. TMEM67 mutations → COACH syndrome (Coloboma, Oligophrenia, Ataxia, Cerebellar vermis hypoplasia, Hepatic fibrosis).
Breathing: Neonatal breathing dysregulation (episodic hyperpnea alternating with apnea) is pathognomonic when present but may be subtle. Pulse oximetry monitoring in neonatal period. Usually improves with age.
Genetics: Send comprehensive JSRD panel (40+ genes) or WES. Single-gene sequencing is low yield. Prenatal diagnosis and genetic counseling for future pregnancies.

Craniosynostosis

Craniosynostosis — premature fusion of one or more cranial sutures — produces characteristic head shape abnormalities and carries risk of elevated intracranial pressure, especially in multi-suture and syndromic forms. The majority of syndromic craniosynostoses involve gain-of-function mutations in FGFR1, FGFR2, or FGFR3 (fibroblast growth factor receptors), with downstream effects on cranial suture ossification timing.

Table 6. Syndromic Craniosynostosis

SYNDROMEGENE / PATHWAYSUTURES FUSEDHEAD SHAPEASSOCIATED FEATURES / NEUROLOGY PEARLS
Non-syndromic (isolated)Variable; TWIST1, EFNB1 in someSingle suture most common: sagittal (scaphocephaly, ~50%), coronal (plagiocephaly), metopic (trigonocephaly), lambdoidScaphocephaly (sagittal); anterior plagiocephaly (coronal); trigonocephaly (metopic); posterior plagiocephaly (lambdoid — distinguish from deformational)Most isolated single-suture synostosis: normal neurodevelopment. Elevated ICP more common in multi-suture. Neurology role: distinguish craniosynostosis from positional plagiocephaly (no suture fusion, no ICP risk). Signs of raised ICP: papilledema, sun-setting, behavioral change, headache.
Apert syndromeFGFR2 p.Ser252Trp or p.Pro253Arg (gain-of-function)Bicoronal (brachycephaly); may involve other suturesBrachycephaly; turribrachycephaly; midface hypoplasia; hypertelorismComplex syndactyly hands/feet (pathognomonic — 'mitten hands'); choanal atresia; cleft palate; intellectual disability (variable; ~50% moderate-severe); ACC and other CNS anomalies in ~10%. One of the more severe craniosynostosis syndromes neurologically. De novo in most.
Crouzon syndromeFGFR2 (many variants); rarely FGFR3Multi-suture (bicoronal most common); variableBrachycephaly; midface hypoplasia; exorbitism (proptosis); beaked noseNormal intelligence in most (key distinction from Apert). Risk of elevated ICP, obstructive sleep apnea (midface hypoplasia), Chiari I (cervical spine anomalies). Autosomal dominant — high penetrance, variable expressivity. Ophthalmology follow-up essential.
Muenke syndromeFGFR3 p.Pro250Arg (single recurrent mutation — most common syndromic craniosynostosis)Unicoronal or bicoronal; occasionally coronal variant without synostosisAnterior plagiocephaly (unicoronal); brachycephaly (bicoronal)Most common syndromic craniosynostosis. Variable expressivity — same mutation causes unicoronal synostosis in one family member, learning disability without synostosis in another. Sensorineural hearing loss (~30%). Mild developmental delay in some. Autosomal dominant. Distinguish from non-syndromic coronal synostosis by FGFR3 testing.
Pfeiffer syndromeFGFR1 p.Pro252Arg (type 1); FGFR2 (types 2/3)Multi-suture; bicoronal predominantBrachycephaly; turribrachycephaly (severe forms); Kleeblattschädel (cloverleaf skull) in type 2/3Broad thumbs/toes (pathognomonic). Type 1 (FGFR1): mild, normal intelligence. Types 2/3 (FGFR2): severe — cloverleaf skull, proptosis, airway compromise, hydrocephalus, significant intellectual disability. Chiari I/II association in severe forms. Neurosurgical and multidisciplinary craniofacial team management.
Carpenter syndromeRAB23 (most); MEGF8Multi-sutureAcrocephaly; variablePoly/syndactyly; obesity; hypogonadism; intellectual disability (variable). CNS anomalies including ACC. Autosomal recessive — unlike most other syndromic craniosynostoses. Rare.
Craniosynostosis — Neurology Pearls
Deformational vs. synostotic plagiocephaly: deformational posterior plagiocephaly (positional) is common (no suture fusion, no ICP risk, parallelogram-shaped head, ipsilateral ear anterior); lambdoid synostosis is rare (trapezoid head, ipsilateral ear posterior, mastoid bulge, palpable ridge). The distinction matters — deformational requires repositioning/helmet, not surgery.
Elevated ICP signs in craniosynostosis: papilledema (requires formal ophthalmologic exam), sun-setting gaze, behavioral change, headache, nausea/vomiting, accelerating head growth. All children with multi-suture synostosis should have ophthalmologic screening.
Chiari I association: seen in Crouzon, Pfeiffer, and other FGFR-related syndromes — likely due to posterior fossa underdevelopment. Screen with MRI.
Muenke syndrome: FGFR3 p.Pro250Arg is a single recurrent mutation worth knowing — it is the most common single-gene cause of syndromic craniosynostosis. SNHL in ~30% warrants audiology referral.

Neurocutaneous Disorders

NF1 Diagnostic Criteria

Neurofibromatosis Type 1 — need ≥2 of the following criteria
Parent with NF1
Six or more café‑au‑lait macules (>5 mm prepubertal; >15 mm postpubertal)
Axillary or inguinal freckling
Two or more neurofibromas of any type OR one plexiform neurofibroma
Optic pathway glioma
Two or more Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas)
Distinctive osseous lesion (e.g., sphenoid wing dysplasia, cortical thinning ± pseudarthrosis)
Genetics: autosomal dominant; NF1, 17q.

Neurofibromatosis Type 2 (NF2)

Genetics: AD, NF2 (22q).

Bilateral vestibular schwannomas; meningiomas, spinal ependymomas, early cataracts.

Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC)

Genetics: AD; TSC1 (hamartin) and TSC2 (tuberin).

CNS: cortical tubers, radial migration lines, subependymal nodules, SEGAs; epilepsy (incl. infantile spasms), ID, ASD.

Systemic: renal angiomyolipomas/cysts, cardiac rhabdomyomas, pulmonary LAM, characteristic skin lesions.

Screening: periodic brain MRI, renal imaging, echo/ECG, chest imaging in at‑risk adolescents/adults.

Treatment: vigabatrin for spasms; ASMs and epilepsy surgery; mTOR inhibitors for SEGAs and some seizure phenotypes.

TSC Diagnostic Criteria

Level of certaintyDefinition
Definite TSC2 major features or 1 major feature + ≥2 minor features
Probable TSC1 major feature + 1 minor feature
Possible TSC1 major feature or ≥2 minor features
Major featuresMinor features
Facial angiofibromas or forehead plaqueMultiple randomly distributed pits in dental enamel
Hypomelanotic macules (≥3)Hamartomatous rectal polyps
Shagreen patchBone cysts
Ungual or periungual fibromaCerebral white‑matter migration lines
Multiple retinal nodular hamartomasGingival fibromas
Cortical tuberNon‑renal hamartoma
Subependymal noduleRetinal achromic patch
Subependymal giant cell astrocytoma"Confetti" skin lesions
Cardiac rhabdomyoma, single or multipleMultiple renal cysts
Lymphangiomyomatosis
Renal angiomyolipoma

Sturge–Weber Syndrome

Somatic activating GNAQ mutation (sporadic).

Unilateral facial port‑wine stain (V1 distribution); leptomeningeal angioma; glaucoma/buphthalmos.

Seizures, hemiparesis, visual field deficits, cognitive impairment.

"Tram‑track" cortical calcifications on CT.

Other Neurocutaneous Reminders

  • Incontinentia pigmenti – vesicular rash → streaky hyperpigmentation; seizures and developmental issues.
  • Hypomelanosis of Ito – whorled/linear hypopigmentation; often seizures and ID.
  • Ataxia telangiectasia – progressive ataxia, telangiectasias, immunodeficiency, elevated AFP, malignancy risk.
  • Gorlin syndrome – basal cell carcinomas, jaw cysts, desmoplastic medulloblastoma.

Neuro‑Metabolic Disorders: General Approach

Conceptual Framework

Most neurometabolic disorders can be organized along two fundamental axes: biochemical class (what is biochemically wrong) and clinical tempo (how they present over time). Overlaid on this is the question of which organ systems are involved and what the MRI shows. Getting these three axes right guides both the urgency and the direction of your workup.

The Core Dichotomy: Small-Molecule vs. Large-Molecule

Small-molecule (intoxication/energy) disorders involve the accumulation of toxic small metabolites or failure of energy production. Because these metabolites are water-soluble and can be cleared — by dialysis, dietary manipulation, cofactors, or specific medications — many are highly treatable. This is the group you cannot miss.

Large-molecule (organelle) disorders involve failure to process complex molecules within organelles (lysosomes, peroxisomes, Golgi). Damage accumulates structurally over time. With limited exceptions (early enzyme replacement, HSC transplant in selected cases), these are less immediately reversible, and the clinical course is typically progressive.

A Critical Nuance: Intoxication vs. Energy Failure

Within the small-molecule category, intoxication-type disorders (e.g., organic acidemias, UCDs, MSUD) and energy-failure disorders (e.g., FAODs, mitochondrial RC defects, GLUT1) are distinct. This matters clinically: organic acidemias cause toxicity from accumulating organic acids and often produce ketosis and metabolic acidosis; FAODs primarily cause energy failure during fasting and produce hypoketotic hypoglycemia. Mitochondrial disorders can present with either pattern or both. Recognizing the sub-type guides initial empirical management.

Why Some IEMs Cause Episodic Disease and Others Cause Steady Decline

This depends on whether the pathological mechanism is acute metabolite accumulation (reversible if treated) vs. structural or irreversible cellular damage over time. In UCD or MSUD, ammonia or leucine rises during metabolic stress, causing encephalopathy — but the neuron itself can recover if the crisis resolves. In contrast, lysosomal storage with progressive lipid or substrate deposition is structural; neurons die. Many disorders are intermediate: basal ganglia injury in organic acidemias (irreversible stroke-like damage) can occur against a backdrop of episodic presentation.

Phenotypic Spectrum and Residual Enzyme Activity

The same gene can produce wildly different phenotypes depending on residual enzyme activity or variant severity. Classic PKU with near-zero PAH activity causes severe untreated intellectual disability; mild hyperphenylalaninemia from a different variant may be asymptomatic. Infantile Pompe (GAA) causes fatal cardiomyopathy in infancy; late-onset Pompe causes a slowly progressive limb-girdle/respiratory myopathy in adulthood. Residents should resist anchoring to the textbook presentation of a named disorder — the phenotypic range of most IEMs is broader than the classic description.

Table 1. The Three-Axis Framework

AXISSMALL-MOLECULE / INTOXICATION-ENERGYLARGE-MOLECULE / ORGANELLE
Biochemical ClassAminoacidopathies, organic acidemias, UCDs, FAODs, small-molecule mitochondrial defects. Accumulation of toxic small metabolites or failure of energy production.Lysosomal storage disorders, peroxisomal disorders, CDGs, glycogen storage disorders. Failure to process complex molecules within organelles.
Clinical TempoAcute/episodic encephalopathy, seizures, vomiting, ataxia, coma, stroke-like episodes. Often triggered by illness, fasting, protein load, surgery, or new medications.Insidious developmental regression, progressive neurodegeneration. Typically no acute triggers; may have chronic progressive or subacute-regressive course.
Systemic FeaturesHyperammonemia, metabolic acidosis, hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis, ketosis or hypoketosis, cardiomyopathy, rhabdomyolysis, liver failure.Coarse facies, hepatosplenomegaly, corneal clouding, cherry-red spot, bone disease, skin/hair changes, dysmorphic features.
MRI PatternOften normal or subtle early. Cortical/basal ganglia edema or diffusion restriction during crises. Symmetric deep gray involvement in energy defects. Pattern may normalize between episodes.Symmetric leukodystrophy, cerebellar/brainstem atrophy, progressive cortical or deep gray volume loss. White matter pattern often characteristic and disease-specific.
ReversibilityOften partially reversible with acute intervention and dietary/cofactor treatment. Many are highly treatable — this is the group NOT to miss.Generally irreversible structural damage. Focus is on slowing progression, enzyme replacement, substrate reduction, or HSC transplant where available.
Kentucky NBSMany captured: PKU, MSUD, PA, MMA, IVA, GA1, HCY, MCAD, VLCAD, LCHAD, CPT1/2, others. A normal NBS does NOT exclude all IEMs in this category.Few captured: only Krabbe (added KY 2016), Pompe, Gaucher, Fabry, MPS-I, MPS-II, NPC (some states). Most LSDs require clinical suspicion and targeted testing.

Pattern Recognition: Clinical Clues and Pitfalls

The following table summarizes high-yield clinical presentations with the most important diagnostic considerations and practical pitfalls. A normal MRI does not exclude an IEM. A normal newborn screen does not exclude an IEM.

Table 2. Clinical Clue–Diagnosis Pairings and Pitfalls

CLINICAL FINDINGTHINK ABOUTKEY POINT / PITFALL
Acute encephalopathy + hyperammonemiaUCD, organic acidemia, FAOD (esp. with liver failure)Treat the ammonia — do not wait for a diagnosis. Protein restriction and caloric support immediately.
Lactic acidosis, normal anion gap or elevated L:P ratioMitochondrial disease (RC defect, PDH deficiency)Lactate can normalize between episodes. A single normal value does not exclude mitochondrial disease.
Episodic ataxia / movement crisis ± vomitingMSUD, organic acidemia, mitochondrial, urea cycle, GLUT1 (fasting-provoked)Timing relative to meals and illness is diagnostically critical. Ask specifically.
Regression after febrile illness, normal inter-episode examIntoxication-type IEM (organic acidemia, UCD), Rett syndrome, FIRESPartial recovery between episodes favors small-molecule IEM. Rett: regression typically 12–18 months, girls, hand stereotypies.
Normal MRI in a child with regression or encephalopathyEarly IEM (any class), GLUT1 (especially with ketogenic response), creatine deficiency, NKHA normal MRI does NOT exclude IEM. Many are MRI-negative early — metabolic workup is independent of imaging.
Progressive white matter disease (leukodystrophy)MLD, Krabbe (NBS in KY), X-ALD, Alexander, Canavan, vanishing white matter, LBSL, AGSPattern and distribution narrow the differential significantly. Refer to leukodystrophy classification.
Cherry-red spotGM1/GM2 gangliosidosis, Niemann-Pick A, sialidosis, galactosialidosisAbsence does NOT exclude LSD. Requires dilated exam by experienced examiner.
Hepatosplenomegaly + neurological declineNiemann-Pick C, Gaucher type 3, GM1, MPS disordersNPC: vertical supranuclear gaze palsy is classic but may be subtle early. Filipin staining or NPC1/NPC2 gene testing.
Neonatal seizures refractory to standard AEDsPyridoxine-dependent epilepsy (ALDH7A1), PNPO deficiency, NKH, biotinidase deficiency, molybdenum cofactor deficiencyTrial of pyridoxine (100 mg IV), pyridoxal-5-phosphate, and folinic acid is warranted in refractory neonatal seizures before extensive workup.
Young infant: hypotonia + neurodegeneration + hair/connective tissue abnormalityMenkes disease (ATP7A)Low copper and ceruloplasmin in infancy. X-linked. Early copper-histidinate treatment may help if started pre-symptomatically.
Adolescent: liver disease + basal ganglia signal + psychiatric symptomsWilson disease (ATP7B)Kayser-Fleischer rings absent in ~50% of neurological Wilson's. Serum copper paradoxically low; 24-hr urine copper elevated.
Intellectual disability + movement disorder + absent/low creatine on MRSCreatine synthesis (AGAT, GAMT) or transport defect (SLC6A8)SLC6A8 (X-linked) is among the most common IEMs causing ID. Urine creatine:creatinine ratio is a practical first screen.
Timing of Sample Collection Matters
Organic acids and acylcarnitines are highest yield during a metabolic crisis — collect before dietary changes or treatment when possible.
Ammonia can normalize rapidly. If you suspect a UCD and the child has been NPO or on IVF with dextrose, repeat ammonia after a protein challenge if the first is normal.
Lactate can be spuriously elevated by poor collection technique (tourniquet, delayed processing). A single normal lactate does not exclude mitochondrial disease.
CSF studies for NKH (glycine encephalopathy) require a simultaneous plasma glycine — ratio >0.08 is diagnostic. Collect both at the same time.

Treatable IEMs: Do Not Miss

The following disorders are emphasized because (1) they are treatable — sometimes dramatically so — and (2) missing them results in preventable harm. Kentucky NBS status is noted; a normal NBS does not exclude disorders not on the panel or conditions with poor sensitivity on screen.

Table 3. High-Priority Treatable IEMs — Don't Miss

DISORDERMECHANISMKEY CLINICAL CLUEDON'T-MISS TESTTREATMENTKY NBS
GLUT1 deficiency (SLC2A1)Impaired glucose transport across BBB; CSF glucose low relative to serumSeizures worsened by fasting, improved fasting ketosis; acquired microcephaly; movement disorderFasting CSF:serum glucose ratio (<0.45); SLC2A1 sequencingKetogenic diet (highly effective)No
Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy (ALDH7A1)Deficient P6C-mediated pyridoxal trapping; pipecolic acid accumulatesRefractory neonatal/infantile seizures; response to IV pyridoxine trialUrine/plasma α-AASA; pipecolic acid; ALDH7A1 genePyridoxine + lysine restriction + arginine supplementationNo
Biotinidase deficiencyFailure to recycle biotin; multiple carboxylase deficiencySeizures, alopecia, rash, sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophyBiotinidase enzyme activityBiotin (inexpensive, highly effective if early)Yes (KY)
Creatine deficiency (GAMT, AGAT, SLC6A8)Deficient creatine synthesis or transport to CNS; energy failureIntellectual disability, autism features, epilepsy; absent creatine peak on MRSUrine Cr:creatinine ratio; guanidinoacetate; gene panelCreatine ± ornithine ± arginine (synthesis defects); SLC6A8 responds lessNo
Urea cycle defects (OTC, CPS1, ASS1, others)Failure of nitrogen disposal; ammonia neurotoxicityAcute encephalopathy, cyclic vomiting, protein avoidance; OTC X-linked (males >> females)Ammonia (critical), plasma amino acids, urine orotic acidNitrogen scavengers (benzoate/phenylbutyrate), arginine, low-protein diet, liver transplantYes (ASA, AS — not OTC/CPS1 directly)
MSUD (BCKDH deficiency)Branched-chain keto acid accumulation; cerebral edema, demyelinationNeonatal encephalopathy, maple syrup odor, episodic crisis with illnessPlasma amino acids (leucine/isoleucine/valine elevation); acylcarnitine profileDietary leucine restriction; acute: dialysis for severe crisis; liver TxYes (KY)
Propionic / methylmalonic acidemiaOrganic acid accumulation; mitochondrial toxicity, basal ganglia injuryNeonatal acidosis, hyperammonemia, basal ganglia stroke in crisis, cardiomyopathyUrine organic acids; acylcarnitine (C3); plasma amino acids (glycine elevation)Protein restriction, carnitine, biotin (PA); B12 for responsive MMA; OHCblYes (KY)
Homocystinuria / remethylation defectsExcess homocysteine; vascular, skeletal, ocular, CNS toxicityMarfanoid, lens dislocation (downward), DVT, stroke, intellectual disabilityTotal plasma homocysteine; plasma amino acids; folate, B12; methylation gene panelPyridoxine trial (CBS-responsive); betaine; methionine restriction; folate/B12Yes — classical HCY (NBS for CBS deficiency); not all remethylation defects
Niemann-Pick C (NPC1/NPC2)Impaired intracellular cholesterol trafficking; lysosomal accumulationVertical supranuclear gaze palsy, ataxia, cognitive decline, dystonia; hepatosplenomegalyFilipin staining; plasma oxysterols (7-KC); NPC1/NPC2 geneMiglustat (substrate reduction); supportive; liver Tx not effective for neurological diseaseNo (some states only)
X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy (ABCD1)Peroxisomal VLCFA oxidation failure; inflammatory demyelination (cerebral) or axonopathy (AMN)Boys: cerebral ALD with behavioral/school decline and white matter disease; ACTH stimulation for adrenal insufficiencyVLCFAs (C26:0, C24/C22 ratio); ABCD1 gene; MRI (parieto-occipital WM)HSCT for early cerebral ALD (pre-symptomatic ideal); adrenal replacement; Lorenzo's oil does not halt neurological diseaseYes (KY, males)
Phenylketonuria (PKU)PAH deficiency; phenylalanine neurotoxicity; dopamine/serotonin depletionIntellectual disability, seizures, behavioral issues, tremor, mousy odor (untreated)Plasma amino acids (phenylalanine); PAH genePhe-restricted diet; sapropterin (BH4) for responsive variants; pegvaliase (adults)Yes (KY) — NBS success story; most cases now detected pre-symptomatically

Tiered Workup for Suspected Neurometabolic Disease

First-Tier Screen

Obtain in all children with unexplained global developmental delay, regression, early-onset movement disorder, or recurrent metabolic decompensation — unless a clear alternative diagnosis exists.

Table 4. First-Tier Metabolic Screen

COMPARTMENTTESTWHAT IT SCREENS FOR
BloodAmmoniaUrea cycle defects, organic acidemias, liver failure
BloodLactate (± pyruvate)Mitochondrial disease, PDH deficiency, organic acidemias — NOTE: collect properly (no tourniquet, free-flowing); a single normal value does not exclude mitochondrial disease
BloodPlasma amino acidsAminoacidopathies (PKU, MSUD, HCU), UCD (citrulline, argininosuccinate), NKH (glycine)
BloodAcylcarnitine profileFAODs, organic acidemias, some mitochondrial defects
BloodTotal homocysteineCBS deficiency, remethylation defects (MTHFR, cblC/D/E/G)
BloodGlucose, electrolytes, BMP, LFTs, CBCHypoglycemia, acidosis, liver disease — essential context for interpreting metabolic results
BloodCopper + ceruloplasminMenkes (low Cu, low Cp — most relevant in infancy/early childhood); Wilson (low Cp, ↑24h urine Cu — adolescent)
UrineOrganic acidsOrganic acidemias, MMA, PA, IVA, MCD, FAOD ketone profiles, mitochondrial
UrineUrine amino acids / GAGs / oligosaccharidesMPS disorders (GAGs), oligosaccharidoses, aminoacidopathies
UrineCreatine : creatinine ratio + guanidinoacetateCreatine synthesis defects (GAMT, AGAT), SLC6A8 transport defect
UrinePurines and pyrimidinesLesch-Nyhan, ADSL deficiency, UMP synthase defects, orotic aciduria (OTC deficiency screen)

Second-Tier Targeted Testing

Use first-tier results, clinical pattern, and MRI findings to guide second-tier investigations. Not all of the following are needed in every child — let the phenotype direct the workup.

Table 5. Second-Tier Targeted Testing by Pattern

SCENARIO / PATTERNTARGETED STUDIESNOTES
Suspected peroxisomal disorder (ZSD, RCDP, X-ALD): dysmorphism, cataracts, leukodystrophy, adrenal insufficiencyVLCFAs; plasmalogens; phytanic/pristanic acids; ABCD1 gene for X-ALDX-ALD: males on KY NBS since 2018. ZSD: Zellweger spectrum has broad severity — consider in neonatal hypotonia + cholestasis + dysmorphism.
Suspected LSD: organomegaly, coarse facies, regression, cherry-red spot, bone diseaseLysosomal enzyme panel (leukocytes); urine/plasma GAGs; oligosaccharides; disease-specific substrates (oxysterols for NPC)Enzyme assay is diagnostic for most LSDs. NPC: enzyme activity is normal — requires oxysterols or filipin staining.
Pyridoxine-dependent/AASA spectrum or refractory neonatal seizuresPlasma/urine α-AASA; pipecolic acid; PNPO activity; ALDH7A1 geneEmpirical pyridoxine trial justified before results available in neonatal refractory seizures.
Mitochondrial/energy defect: Leigh syndrome, MERRF, MELAS, complex I–IV deficiencySerum and CSF lactate/pyruvate; acylcarnitines; comprehensive mitochondrial gene panel or WES/WGS (preferred over piecemeal testing)Respiratory chain enzyme studies on tissue (muscle biopsy) still relevant in selected cases where genetics unrevealing. Lactate may be normal in some RC defects.
Cholesterol biosynthesis defect (SLOS, desmosterolosis)Cholesterol and sterol profile; 7-dehydrocholesterol:cholesterol ratioSLOS: 2nd–3rd toe syndactyly, genital anomalies, behavioral phenotype resembling autism.
Neurotransmitter or cerebral folate disorderCSF monoamine metabolites (HVA, 5-HIAA); pterins; 5-MTHF; CSF amino acids (glycine for NKH) — selective useCSF for NKH (nonketotic hyperglycinemia): CSF:plasma glycine ratio > 0.08 is diagnostic. Use CSF studies selectively, not as routine first-tier.
Unexplained regression, unrevealing first-tier screen, no clear biochemical patternExome or genome sequencing (WES/WGS) — early genomic testing preferredSee note below on genomic testing philosophy.
On Genomic Testing: A Philosophy Shift
The older paradigm of exhaustive sequential biochemical testing before genomic workup is outdated for many presentations.
In a child with unexplained developmental regression and an unrevealing first-tier metabolic screen, early WES or WGS is preferred over ordering every conceivable second-tier biochemical test.
Genomic testing does not replace targeted biochemical testing when a specific disorder is suspected or when a biochemical result requires confirmation — but it should not be the test of last resort.
Many IEMs are now diagnosed biochemically only after a genomic result directs attention. The two approaches are complementary.
Exception: when a specific treatable disorder is on the differential (e.g., GLUT1, pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy), do not wait for genomics — treat empirically and test specifically.

Bedside Checklist for the On-Call Resident

Table 6. Stepwise Approach to the Child with Suspected Neurometabolic Disease

STEPACTION
1. StabilizeABCs. Correct hypoglycemia, treat seizures. Do NOT delay acute labs for imaging.
2. Acute labs (all acutely ill children with encephalopathy)Ammonia, blood gas, glucose, lactate, electrolytes, LFTs, CBC, ketones, plasma amino acids, acylcarnitines, urine organic acids (± orotic acid). Treat hyperammonemia and acidosis empirically while pending.
3. Categorize by axesSmall- vs large-molecule. Acute/episodic vs subacute/regressive vs chronic progressive. Multi-organ vs brain-predominant. MRI pattern and head circumference trajectory.
4. Check NBS historyWas NBS performed? What panel (KY)? Normal NBS does NOT exclude IEMs not on the panel or disorders with poor screening sensitivity. Confirm with metabolic lab if NBS was abnormal or incomplete.
5. First-tier metabolic screenIf not yet done, order full panel (see table above). Timing matters: organic acids and acylcarnitines are highest yield DURING a metabolic crisis.
6. Any treatable signal?Patterns suggesting UCD, organic acidemia, aminoacidopathy, creatine defect, pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy, GLUT1, Wilson, Menkes → immediate metabolic/genetics consultation and disease-specific therapy.
7. First-tier unrevealing but suspicion remainsEscalate to targeted second-tier labs based on phenotype and MRI. In a child with unexplained regression and unrevealing biochemical screen, proceed to WES/WGS without delay — exhaustive sequential biochemical testing is NOT required first.

Mitochondrial Disorders

Maternal (mtDNA), autosomal recessive, or autosomal dominant inheritance.

Multisystem involvement; neurologic plus myopathy, cardiomyopathy/arrhythmia, endocrine, GI, renal disease.

Triggers: fever, fasting, infection, some medications (e.g., valproate in suspected POLG disease).

Testing: lactate ± pyruvate, CK, MRI, mtDNA/nuclear panels, RC chain analysis ± biopsy.

Management: avoid mitochondrial toxins; supportive care; CoQ10 or targeted therapies in specific deficiencies.

Recognizable Mitochondrial Syndromes

Syndrome and featuresGenetics
Leigh syndrome – subacute encephalopathy with symmetric brainstem/basal ganglia lesionsAR, mtDNA, or X‑linked
Pearson syndrome – sideroblastic anemia, pancytopenia, exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, renal tubulopathymtDNA
MERRF – myoclonic epilepsy with ragged‑red fibersmtDNA
NARP – neurogenic weakness, ataxia, retinitis pigmentosamtDNA
MELAS – mitochondrial encephalopathy, lactic acidosis, stroke‑like episodesmtDNA
Alpers syndrome – encephalopathy, seizures, hepatic dysfunction (often POLG)Nuclear (AR/AD)
MNGIE – mitochondrial neurogastrointestinal encephalopathyAR
Kearns–Sayre syndrome – CPEO, pigmentary retinopathy, high CSF protein, ataxia, conduction defects (<20 yrs)mtDNA deletions
MIDD – maternally inherited diabetes and deafnessmtDNA
SANDO – sensory ataxia, neuropathy, dysarthria, ophthalmoplegiaAD (nuclear)

Leukodystrophies / Leukoencephalopathies – Pattern Table

DiseaseMRI patternKey testClinical clues

KrabbeCSTs, cerebellar WM, thick nerve rootsGALC enzymeExtreme irritability, early neuropathy, opisthotonos

Developmental Delay ± Epilepsy – Genetic and Metabolic Work‑up

Step 1 – Clinical Triage

  • History: prenatal/perinatal events; regression vs static course; seizure types; family history.
  • Exam: distinctive features (dysmorphism, neurocutaneous signs), head size, organomegaly, movement disorder.
  • MRI brain when focal deficits, abnormal head growth, refractory epilepsy, or concerning exam.

Step 2 – Broad Genetic Testing

If the child meets criteria for global developmental delay (≥2 domains, moderate/severe) with any combination of ID, ASD, epilepsy, abnormal exam, or regression/evolving picture, or is not responding to standard treatment:

  • Order trio WES/WGS as the primary test.
  • Add targeted or syndrome‑specific panels only when clearly indicated.

Step 3 – Additional Studies and Follow‑up

  • Chromosomal microarray (if not already done).
  • Fragile X testing (especially males or suggestive family history).
  • Metabolic testing that may have been missed, guided by phenotype.
  • Consider EEG to evaluate for ESES or other epileptic encephalopathy patterns.
  • Consider repeat MRI if concern for evolving leukodystrophy or basal‑ganglia changes.
  • Refer to a Neurogenetics clinic when expedited, comprehensive evaluation or VUS resolution is needed.

Genetic Counseling, Broad Testing, and GINA

Why Genetic Counseling Is Needed

Broad testing (WES/WGS, large panels) should be ordered with pre‑test counseling by a genetics professional.

Counseling covers possible outcomes (positive, negative, VUS), test limitations, family implications, and the chance of unexpected findings (secondary findings, consanguinity, non‑paternity).

Result Types

  • Positive – pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant explaining the phenotype; guides management and family testing.
  • Negative – no causative variant found; a genetic cause may still exist.
  • Variant of uncertain significance (VUS) – uncertain; should not usually change management. Most reclassified VUS move downward toward benign/likely benign rather than toward pathogenic.

GINA – Key Points for Families (U.S.)

  • GINA limits how health insurers and most employers can use genetic information.
  • Health insurers cannot use genetic test results to set eligibility or premiums or require testing.
  • Employers with ≥15 employees cannot use genetic information in hiring, firing, or promotion decisions.
  • GINA does not cover life, disability, or long‑term‑care insurance, and does not apply to very small employers; families should understand these limits when deciding about testing.
